title: Daumesdick
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 1, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 37. Text from Wikisource („Daumesdick (1857)“, twice proofread against the original). Fresh translation made directly from the German; 1857 spelling preserved exactly.
about: The thirty-seventh tale (KHM 37), the German Tom Thumb. A poor couple’s wish for a child, however small, is granted in a boy no bigger than a thumb, whose cleverness carries him through the horse’s ear, a cow’s belly, a wolf’s stomach and safely home. Older 1857 forms such as ward, gieng, Scheere and Reichthümer appear throughout.

sentences.t: Es war ein armer Bauersmann, der saß Abends beim Herd und schürte das Feuer, und die Frau saß und spann.
sentences.l: There was a poor peasant-man, who sat of an evening by the hearth and stoked the fire, and the wife sat and spun.
sentences.i: There was once a poor peasant who sat of an evening by the hearth and stoked the fire, while his wife sat and spun.
sentences.n: “der saß” resumes “Bauersmann” with a demonstrative der (‘who sat’) — German often points back to a noun with der/die/das rather than a plain relative; you will see this constantly in Grimm. “Abends” (with the old capital and adverbial -s) = ‘of an evening.’ “schürte” is the preterite of schüren, to poke or stoke a fire.

sentences.t: Da sprach er „wie ists so traurig, daß wir keine Kinder haben! es ist so still bei uns, und in den andern Häusern ists so laut und lustig.“
sentences.l: Then he said, “how is it so sad, that we have no children! it is so quiet with us, and in the other houses it is so loud and merry.”
sentences.i: Then he said, “how sad it is that we have no children! it is so quiet in our house, while in the other houses it is so loud and merry.”
sentences.n: “ists” = ist es and “ists” again for it is; these clipped contractions (ists, wenns, ers, wirs) are everywhere in Grimm’s spoken passages. “daß” is the 1857 spelling of dass, kept throughout this edition.

sentences.t: „Ja,“ antwortete die Frau und seufzte, „wenns nur ein einziges wäre, und wenns auch ganz klein wäre, nur Daumens groß, so wollt ich schon zufrieden sein; wir hättens doch von Herzen lieb.“
sentences.l: “Yes,” answered the wife and sighed, “if it were but a single one, and even if it were quite small, only a thumb’s size, then I would be content indeed; we would love it from the heart nonetheless.”
sentences.i: “Yes,” answered the wife with a sigh, “if there were only one, even if it were quite tiny, no bigger than a thumb, I should be well content; we would love it dearly all the same.”
sentences.n: “wenns ... wäre” is the wishing subjunctive (subjunctive II): ‘if only it were.’ “Daumens groß” = ‘a thumb’s size’ (genitive of measure). “hättens ... lieb” = hätten es lieb, ‘would hold it dear’ — lieb haben = to love.

sentences.t: Nun geschah es, daß die Frau kränklich ward und nach sieben Monaten ein Kind gebar, das zwar an allen Gliedern vollkommen aber nicht länger als ein Daumen war.
sentences.l: Now it happened, that the wife grew sickly and after seven months bore a child, that was indeed perfect in every limb but no longer than a thumb.
sentences.i: Now it came to pass that the wife grew sickly, and after seven months she bore a child that was perfect in every limb, yet no longer than a thumb.
sentences.n: “ward” is the archaic preterite of werden (‘became, grew’); modern German uses wurde, but ward runs all through this edition. “zwar ... aber” frames a concession: ‘to be sure ... but.’

sentences.t: Da sprachen sie „es ist wie wir es gewünscht haben, und es soll unser liebes Kind sein,“ und nannten es nach seiner Gestalt Daumesdick.
sentences.l: Then they said, “it is as we have wished it, and it shall be our dear child,” and named it after its shape Daumesdick.
sentences.i: Then they said, “it is just as we wished, and it shall be our dear child,” and after its shape they named it Thumbling.
sentences.n: The name Daumesdick means literally ‘thumb-thick,’ i.e. a boy the size of a thumb; it is left untranslated in the text and glossed here.

sentences.t: Sie ließens nicht an Nahrung fehlen, aber das Kind ward nicht größer, sondern blieb wie es in der ersten Stunde gewesen war; doch schaute es verständig aus den Augen, und zeigte sich bald als ein kluges und behendes Ding, dem alles glückte was es anfieng.
sentences.l: They let it not want for nourishment, but the child grew no bigger, but rather stayed as it had been in the first hour; yet it looked out sensibly from its eyes, and soon showed itself as a clever and nimble thing, to whom everything succeeded that it began.
sentences.i: They let it want for no nourishment, but the child grew no bigger and stayed just as it had been in its first hour; yet it looked out with sensible eyes and soon showed itself a clever, nimble thing, at whose hand everything it began prospered.
sentences.n: “ließens ... fehlen” = ließen es an Nahrung fehlen; ‘an etwas fehlen lassen’ = to let (someone) want for something. “dem alles glückte” — glücken is impersonal and takes the dative: ‘to whom all succeeded.’ “anfieng” is the 1857 -ie- spelling of anfing (anfangen, to begin; separable an-).

sentences.t: Der Bauer machte sich eines Tages fertig in den Wald zu gehen und Holz zu fällen, da sprach er so vor sich hin „nun wollt ich daß einer da wäre, der mir den Wagen nachbrächte.“
sentences.l: The peasant made himself ready one day to go into the forest and to fell wood, then he said thus to himself, “now I would wish that someone were there, who would bring me the wagon after.”
sentences.i: One day the peasant got himself ready to go into the forest and cut wood, and he said half to himself, “now I do wish there were someone to bring the wagon along after me.”
sentences.n: “so vor sich hin sprechen” = to talk half to oneself, musing aloud. “nachbrächte” is subjunctive II of nachbringen (‘would bring after’; separable nach-) — the wished-for helper.

sentences.t: „O Vater,“ rief Daumesdick, „den Wagen will ich schon bringen, verlaßt euch drauf, er soll zur bestimmten Zeit im Walde sein.“
sentences.l: “O father,” cried Daumesdick, “the wagon I will bring right enough, rely yourselves upon it, it shall be in the forest at the appointed time.”
sentences.i: “O father,” cried Thumbling, “I’ll bring the wagon, never fear; it shall be in the forest at the appointed time.”
sentences.n: “will ich schon” — the little word schon here reassures: ‘I’ll manage it all right.’ “verlaßt euch drauf” = verlasst euch darauf, ‘rely on it’ (the parents are addressed with the plural euch).

sentences.t: Da lachte der Mann und sprach „wie sollte das zugehen, du bist viel zu klein, um das Pferd mit dem Zügel zu leiten.“
sentences.l: Then the man laughed and said, “how should that come about, you are far too small to guide the horse with the rein.”
sentences.i: Then the man laughed and said, “how should that ever work — you are far too small to guide the horse by the rein.”
sentences.n: “wie sollte das zugehen” — zugehen = to come about, happen; ‘how is that to work?’ “um ... zu leiten” is the purpose construction ‘in order to guide.’

sentences.t: „Das thut nichts, Vater, wenn nur die Mutter anspannen will, ich setze mich dem Pferd ins Ohr und rufe ihm zu wie es gehen soll.“
sentences.l: “That does nothing, father, if only mother will harness up, I set myself into the horse’s ear and call to it how it should go.”
sentences.i: “That does not matter, father; if only mother will harness the horse, I’ll sit in its ear and call out to it which way to go.”
sentences.n: “das thut nichts” (thut = 1857 spelling of tut) = ‘that doesn’t matter.’ “setze mich dem Pferd ins Ohr” uses the dative dem Pferd of the possessor — ‘into the horse’s ear.’ “rufe ihm zu” is separable zurufen, to call out to (someone).

sentences.t: „Nun,“ antwortete der Vater, „einmal wollen wirs versuchen.“
sentences.l: “Well,” answered the father, “for once we will try it.”
sentences.i: “Well,” answered the father, “we’ll give it a try for once.”
sentences.n: “wollen wirs versuchen” = wollen wir es versuchen (wirs = wir es).

sentences.t: Als die Stunde kam, spannte die Mutter an und setzte Daumesdick ins Ohr des Pferdes, und dann rief der Kleine, wie das Pferd gehen sollte, „jüh und joh! hott und har!“
sentences.l: When the hour came, the mother harnessed up and set Daumesdick into the horse’s ear, and then the little one called, how the horse should go, “jüh and joh! hott and har!”
sentences.i: When the hour came, the mother harnessed the horse and set Thumbling in its ear, and then the little fellow called out how the horse should go: “jüh and joh! hott and har!”
sentences.n: “spannte ... an” is separable anspannen, to harness (a horse to a cart). jüh, joh, hott, har are traditional carter’s calls to a draught horse: jüh and joh urge it onward, while hott and har tell it which way to turn (by common convention hott to the right, har to the left, though usage varied by region). They are all but untranslatable and are kept as they stand.

sentences.t: Da gieng es ganz ordentlich als wie bei einem Meister, und der Wagen fuhr den rechten Weg nach dem Walde.
sentences.l: Then it went quite properly as if by a master, and the wagon drove the right way toward the forest.
sentences.i: And it all went as neatly as if a master were driving, and the wagon rolled straight along the right road to the forest.
sentences.n: “gieng” is the 1857 -ie- spelling of ging (gehen). “als wie” is an old doubled comparative, ‘just as, as if.’

sentences.t: Es trug sich zu, als er eben um eine Ecke bog, und der Kleine „har, har!“ rief, daß zwei fremde Männer daher kamen.
sentences.l: It came to pass, just as he was turning round a corner, and the little one cried “har, har!”, that two strange men came along.
sentences.i: It happened, just as he was rounding a corner and the little fellow was calling “har, har!”, that two strangers came by.
sentences.n: “es trug sich zu ... daß” = ‘it came about that’ (sich zutragen, to come to pass). “fremde Männer” = strangers, unknown men.

sentences.t: „Mein,“ sprach der eine, „was ist das? da fährt ein Wagen, und ein Fuhrmann ruft dem Pferde zu, und ist doch nicht zu sehen.“
sentences.l: “My,” said the one, “what is that? there drives a wagon, and a carter calls to the horse, and yet is not to be seen.”
sentences.i: “Bless me,” said the one, “what is this? there goes a wagon, and a carter is calling to the horse, and yet no one is to be seen.”
sentences.n: “Mein” here is a mild exclamation of surprise (‘my!’, short for ‘mein Gott’). “ist doch nicht zu sehen” — the sein + zu + infinitive construction = ‘is not to be seen, cannot be seen.’

sentences.t: „Das geht nicht mit rechten Dingen zu,“ sagte der andere, „wir wollen dem Karren folgen und sehen wo er anhält.“
sentences.l: “That does not go about by right things,” said the other, “we will follow the cart and see where it halts.”
sentences.i: “There’s something uncanny about this,” said the other; “let us follow the cart and see where it stops.”
sentences.n: “nicht mit rechten Dingen zugehen” is a fixed idiom = ‘to not be aboveboard, to be uncanny / bewitched.’ “anhält” is separable anhalten, to stop, halt.

sentences.t: Der Wagen aber fuhr vollends in den Wald hinein und richtig zu dem Platze, wo das Holz gehauen ward.
sentences.l: The wagon however drove right on into the forest and, sure enough, to the place where the wood was being hewn.
sentences.i: But the wagon drove right on into the forest, and sure enough straight to the place where the wood was being cut.
sentences.n: “vollends” = right on, all the way; “richtig” here = ‘sure enough, just so.’ “gehauen ward” = was hewn (hauen, to cut/chop; ward = archaic passive auxiliary).

sentences.t: Als Daumesdick seinen Vater erblickte, rief er ihm zu „siehst du, Vater, da bin ich mit dem Wagen, nun hol mich herunter.“
sentences.l: When Daumesdick caught sight of his father, he called to him, “do you see, father, here I am with the wagon, now fetch me down.”
sentences.i: When Thumbling caught sight of his father, he called out to him, “you see, father, here I am with the wagon — now take me down.”
sentences.n: “erblickte” = caught sight of, spied. “hol mich herunter” = fetch me down (herunterholen, separable herunter-).

sentences.t: Der Vater faßte das Pferd mit der linken, und holte mit der rechten sein Söhnlein aus dem Ohr, das sich ganz lustig auf einen Strohhalm niedersetzte.
sentences.l: The father grasped the horse with the left, and fetched with the right his little son out of the ear, who quite merrily sat himself down on a straw.
sentences.i: The father seized the horse with his left hand and with his right lifted his little son out of the ear, and the boy sat himself down quite merrily on a wisp of straw.
sentences.n: “mit der linken / mit der rechten” — Hand is understood: with the left / right hand. “Söhnlein” is the -lein diminutive of Sohn, ‘little son’ (Grimm loves such diminutives). “niedersetzte” is separable sich niedersetzen, to sit down.

sentences.t: Als die beiden fremden Männer den Daumesdick erblickten, wußten sie nicht was sie vor Verwunderung sagen sollten.
sentences.l: When the two strange men caught sight of Daumesdick, they knew not what they, for astonishment, should say.
sentences.i: When the two strangers caught sight of Thumbling, they did not know what to say for sheer astonishment.
sentences.n: “vor Verwunderung” — vor + dative here gives the cause, ‘for / out of astonishment’; German uses vor this way for overwhelming feelings (vor Schrecken, vor Freude).

sentences.t: Da nahm der eine den andern beiseit und sprach „hör, der kleine Kerl könnte unser Glück machen, wenn wir ihn in einer großen Stadt vor Geld sehen ließen: wir wollen ihn kaufen.“
sentences.l: Then the one took the other aside and said, “hear, the little fellow could make our fortune, if we let him be seen for money in a great city: we will buy him.”
sentences.i: Then the one drew the other aside and said, “listen, the little fellow could make our fortune if we let people see him for money in some great city — let us buy him.”
sentences.n: “unser Glück machen” = to make our fortune. “vor Geld sehen ließen” = ‘let (him) be seen for money’ (sehen lassen, to let be seen). beiseit is the archaic short form of beiseite, ‘aside.’

sentences.t: Sie giengen zu dem Bauer und sprachen „verkauft uns den kleinen Mann, er solls gut bei uns haben.“
sentences.l: They went to the peasant and said, “sell us the little man, he shall have it good with us.”
sentences.i: They went to the peasant and said, “sell us the little man; he shall be well off with us.”
sentences.n: “er solls gut ... haben” = er soll es gut haben, ‘he shall have a good time of it, be well off.’ verkauft is the plural imperative, ‘sell (ye).’

sentences.t: „Nein,“ antwortete der Vater, „es ist mein Herzblatt, und ist mir für alles Gold in der Welt nicht feil.“
sentences.l: “No,” answered the father, “it is my heart’s-darling, and is to me for all the gold in the world not for sale.”
sentences.i: “No,” answered the father, “he is the darling of my heart, and not for sale to me for all the gold in the world.”
sentences.n: “Herzblatt” (lit. ‘heart-leaf’) is a term of endearment, ‘darling, treasure.’ “feil” is an old word = for sale, purchasable; “mir ... nicht feil” = ‘not for sale as far as I am concerned.’

sentences.t: Daumesdick aber, als er von dem Handel gehört, war an den Rockfalten seines Vaters hinaufgekrochen, stellte sich ihm auf die Schulter, und wisperte ihm ins Ohr „Vater, gib mich nur hin, ich will schon wieder zurück kommen.“
sentences.l: But Daumesdick, when he had heard of the bargain, had crept up the coat-skirts of his father, placed himself on his shoulder, and whispered into his ear, “father, just give me over, I will surely come back again.”
sentences.i: But Thumbling, when he had heard of the deal, had crept up his father’s coat-skirts, set himself on his shoulder, and whispered in his ear, “father, only give me over — I shall surely come back again.”
sentences.n: “Rockfalten” = the folds or skirts of a coat (Rock here in the old sense of a man’s coat). “gib mich nur hin” — hingeben, to give over/hand over; the little nur softens it, ‘just give me over.’ “ich will schon” — schon again reassuring: ‘I’ll come back all right.’

sentences.t: Da gab ihn der Vater für ein schönes Stück Geld den beiden Männern hin.
sentences.l: Then the father gave him over for a fine piece of money to the two men.
sentences.i: So the father handed him over to the two men for a fine sum of money.
sentences.n: “gab ... hin” is separable hingeben, to hand over — the prefix hin jumps to the end. “ein schönes Stück Geld” = ‘a tidy sum.’

sentences.t: „Wo willst du sitzen?“ sprachen sie zu ihm.
sentences.l: “Where will you sit?” said they to him.
sentences.i: “Where would you like to sit?” they said to him.

sentences.t: „Ach, setzt mich nur auf den Rand von eurem Hut, da kann ich auf und ab spazieren und die Gegend betrachten, und falle doch nicht herunter.“
sentences.l: “Oh, just set me on the rim of your hat, there I can stroll up and down and view the countryside, and yet do not fall down.”
sentences.i: “Oh, just set me on the brim of your hat; there I can stroll up and down and look at the countryside, and still not fall off.”
sentences.n: “auf und ab spazieren” = to stroll up and down. “falle ... herunter” — herunterfallen, to fall down (separable herunter-); doch here = ‘still, all the same.’

sentences.t: Sie thaten ihm den Willen, und als Daumesdick Abschied von seinem Vater genommen hatte, machten sie sich mit ihm fort.
sentences.l: They did him the will, and when Daumesdick had taken leave of his father, they made themselves off with him.
sentences.i: They did as he wished, and when Thumbling had taken leave of his father, they set off with him.
sentences.n: “jemandem den Willen thun” = to do as someone wishes (thaten = 1857 taten). “Abschied nehmen” = to take one’s leave. “sich fortmachen” = to make off, set off (separable fort-).

sentences.t: So giengen sie bis es dämmerig ward, da sprach der Kleine „hebt mich einmal herunter, es ist nöthig.“
sentences.l: So they went until it grew dusky, then the little one said, “lift me down for a moment, it is necessary.”
sentences.i: So they walked on until it grew dusk, and then the little fellow said, “lift me down a moment — I need to.”
sentences.n: “es ist nöthig” (nöthig = 1857 nötig) is a discreet way of saying he needs to relieve himself. “hebt mich herunter” = herunterheben, lift down (separable herunter-).

sentences.t: „Bleib nur droben,“ sprach der Mann, auf dessen Kopf er saß, „ich will mir nichts draus machen, die Vögel lassen mir auch manchmal was drauf fallen.“
sentences.l: “Just stay up there,” said the man, on whose head he sat, “I will make nothing of it for myself, the birds too sometimes let something fall on it for me.”
sentences.i: “Just stay up there,” said the man on whose head he was sitting; “I shan’t mind it — the birds too drop something on it now and then.”
sentences.n: “sich nichts draus machen” = daraus machen, ‘to think nothing of it, not mind it.’ “lassen mir ... was drauf fallen” = darauf, ‘let something fall on it.’

sentences.t: „Nein,“ sprach Daumesdick, „ich weiß auch, was sich schickt: hebt mich nur geschwind herab.“
sentences.l: “No,” said Daumesdick, “I too know what is fitting: just lift me down quickly.”
sentences.i: “No,” said Thumbling, “I know very well what is proper — only lift me down quickly.”
sentences.n: “was sich schickt” — sich schicken = to be fitting, seemly; ‘what is proper.’ “herab” = down (from up above); herabheben, to lift down.

sentences.t: Der Mann nahm den Hut ab, und setzte den Kleinen auf einen Acker am Weg, da sprang und kroch er ein wenig zwischen den Schollen hin und her, dann schlüpfte er plötzlich in ein Mausloch, das er sich ausgesucht hatte.
sentences.l: The man took off the hat, and set the little one on a field by the way, there he jumped and crept a little among the clods to and fro, then he slipped suddenly into a mouse-hole, that he had picked out for himself.
sentences.i: The man took off his hat and set the little fellow on a field beside the road; there he sprang and crept about a little among the clods, then suddenly slipped into a mouse-hole he had picked out for himself.
sentences.n: “nahm ... ab” = abnehmen, to take off (separable ab-). “hin und her” = to and fro. “ausgesucht” = aussuchen, to pick out, choose (separable aus-).

sentences.t: „Guten Abend, ihr Herren, geht nur ohne mich heim,“ rief er ihnen zu, und lachte sie aus.
sentences.l: “Good evening, you gentlemen, just go home without me,” he called to them, and laughed them out.
sentences.i: “Good evening, my masters, just go home without me,” he called out to them, and laughed them to scorn.
sentences.n: “rief ... zu” = zurufen, to call out to. “lachte sie aus” = auslachen, to laugh at, mock (separable aus-).

sentences.t: Sie liefen herbei und stachen mit Stöcken in das Mausloch, aber das war vergebliche Mühe: Daumesdick kroch immer weiter zurück und da es bald ganz dunkel ward, so mußten sie mit Ärger und mit leerem Beutel wieder heim wandern.
sentences.l: They ran up and jabbed with sticks into the mouse-hole, but that was vain effort: Daumesdick crept ever further back, and since it soon grew quite dark, they had to trudge home again with vexation and with empty purse.
sentences.i: They ran up and jabbed at the mouse-hole with sticks, but it was wasted effort: Thumbling crept further and further back, and as it soon grew quite dark they had to trudge home again in vexation and with an empty purse.
sentences.n: “stachen” is the preterite of stechen, here to jab, poke. “vergebliche Mühe” = vain effort. “mit leerem Beutel” = ‘with an empty purse,’ i.e. out of pocket.

sentences.t: Als Daumesdick merkte daß sie fort waren, kroch er aus dem unterirdischen Gang wieder hervor.
sentences.l: When Daumesdick noticed that they were gone, he crept out of the underground passage again.
sentences.i: When Thumbling saw that they had gone, he crept out again from the underground passage.
sentences.n: “fort waren” = were gone, had left. “kroch ... hervor” = hervorkriechen, to creep out/forth (separable hervor-).

sentences.t: „Es ist auf dem Acker in der Finsternis so gefährlich gehen,“ sprach er, „wie leicht bricht einer Hals und Bein!“
sentences.l: “It is so dangerous to go on the field in the darkness,” he said, “how easily one breaks neck and leg!”
sentences.i: “It is so dangerous walking on the field in the dark,” he said; “how easily one may break one’s neck and leg!”
sentences.n: “Hals und Bein brechen” is a set phrase, ‘to break one’s neck and leg’ (compare the good-luck wish Hals- und Beinbruch). “einer” here = ‘one, a person.’

sentences.t: Zum Glück stieß er an ein leeres Schneckenhaus.
sentences.l: By luck he knocked against an empty snail-shell.
sentences.i: By good fortune he stumbled against an empty snail-shell.
sentences.n: “zum Glück” = luckily, by good fortune. “stieß an” = anstoßen, to bump/knock against (separable an-).

sentences.t: „Gottlob,“ sagte er, „da kann ich die Nacht sicher zubringen,“ und setzte sich hinein.
sentences.l: “Thank God,” he said, “there I can spend the night safely,” and set himself inside.
sentences.i: “Thank God,” he said, “here I can pass the night in safety,” and settled himself inside.
sentences.n: “Gottlob” = ‘thank God’ (from Gott + lob, praise). “zubringen” = to spend, pass (time). “setzte sich hinein” = seated himself inside (hineinsetzen, separable hinein-).

sentences.t: Nicht lang, als er eben einschlafen wollte, so hörte er zwei Männer vorüber gehen, davon sprach der eine „wie wirs nur anfangen, um dem reichen Pfarrer sein Geld und sein Silber zu holen?“
sentences.l: Not long, as he was just about to fall asleep, he heard two men go past, of whom the one said, “how shall we only set about it, to fetch the rich parson his money and his silver?”
sentences.i: Not long after, just as he was about to fall asleep, he heard two men passing by, and one of them said, “however shall we set about getting the rich parson’s money and silver?”
sentences.n: “wie wirs nur anfangen” = wie wir es nur anfangen (anfangen here = to set about, go about a thing). “dem reichen Pfarrer sein Geld” uses the dative dem Pfarrer for the possessor — a colloquial way of saying ‘the rich parson’s money.’

sentences.t: „Das könnt ich dir sagen,“ rief Daumesdick dazwischen.
sentences.l: “That I could tell you,” called Daumesdick in between.
sentences.i: “That I could tell you,” Thumbling called out amid their talk.
sentences.n: “dazwischen ... rufen” = to call out in the middle (of what someone is saying), to chime in.

sentences.t: „Was war das?“ sprach der eine Dieb erschrocken, „ich hörte jemand sprechen.“
sentences.l: “What was that?” said the one thief, startled, “I heard someone speak.”
sentences.i: “What was that?” said the one thief, startled; “I heard someone speaking.”
sentences.n: “erschrocken” is the past participle of erschrecken used adverbially, ‘in fright, startled.’

sentences.t: Sie blieben stehen und horchten, da sprach Daumesdick wieder „nehmt mich mit, so will ich euch helfen.“
sentences.l: They remained standing and listened, then Daumesdick spoke again, “take me along, then I will help you.”
sentences.i: They stopped and listened, and Thumbling spoke again: “take me along, and I will help you.”
sentences.n: “blieben stehen” = stehen bleiben, to stop, halt (lit. ‘remained standing’). “nehmt mich mit” = mitnehmen, to take along (separable mit-).

sentences.t: „Wo bist du denn?“
sentences.l: “Where are you then?”
sentences.i: “But where are you?”
sentences.n: The particle denn in a question adds urgency or wonder: ‘where on earth are you?’

sentences.t: „Sucht nur auf der Erde und merkt wo die Stimme herkommt,“ antwortete er.
sentences.l: “Just search on the ground and mark where the voice comes from,” he answered.
sentences.i: “Just look on the ground and mark where the voice comes from,” he answered.
sentences.n: “merkt wo ... herkommt” — herkommen = to come from; the her- points back toward the speaker.

sentences.t: Da fanden ihn endlich die Diebe und hoben ihn in die Höhe.
sentences.l: Then the thieves at last found him and lifted him up on high.
sentences.i: Then at last the thieves found him and lifted him up.
sentences.n: “in die Höhe heben” = to lift up, raise aloft (hoben is the preterite of heben).

sentences.t: „Du kleiner Wicht, was willst du uns helfen!“ sprachen sie.
sentences.l: “You little wight, what can you help us!” they said.
sentences.i: “You little wretch, how could you help us!” they said.
sentences.n: “Wicht” = a little creature, wretch, ‘wight’ (often half-scornful). “was willst du uns helfen” = ‘what/how are you to help us,’ an incredulous question.

sentences.t: „Seht,“ antwortete er, „ich krieche zwischen den Eisenstäben in die Kammer des Pfarrers und reiche euch heraus was ihr haben wollt.“
sentences.l: “See,” he answered, “I creep between the iron bars into the parson’s chamber and hand out to you what you want to have.”
sentences.i: “Look,” he answered, “I’ll creep between the iron bars into the parson’s chamber and hand out to you whatever you want.”
sentences.n: “Eisenstäben” = iron bars (of a grating). “reiche ... heraus” = herausreichen, to hand out (separable heraus-).

sentences.t: „Wohlan,“ sagten sie, „wir wollen sehen was du kannst.“
sentences.l: “Well then,” they said, “we will see what you can do.”
sentences.i: “Very well,” they said, “let us see what you can do.”
sentences.n: “Wohlan” is an old, slightly elevated interjection = ‘well then, come now.’

sentences.t: Als sie bei dem Pfarrhaus kamen, kroch Daumesdick in die Kammer, schrie aber gleich aus Leibeskräften „wollt ihr alles haben, was hier ist?“
sentences.l: When they came to the parsonage, Daumesdick crept into the chamber, but at once shrieked with all his bodily might, “do you want to have everything that is here?”
sentences.i: When they reached the parsonage, Thumbling crept into the chamber, but at once shouted at the top of his lungs, “do you want everything that’s here?”
sentences.n: “aus Leibeskräften” = with all one’s bodily strength, ‘at the top of one’s lungs.’ Thumbling is deliberately loud to foil the thieves.

sentences.t: Die Diebe erschraken und sagten „so sprich doch leise, damit niemand aufwacht.“
sentences.l: The thieves took fright and said, “do speak softly, so that no one wakes up.”
sentences.i: The thieves were alarmed and said, “do speak softly, so that no one wakes.”
sentences.n: “erschraken” is the preterite of erschrecken (intransitive), ‘took fright.’ “sprich doch leise” — the particle doch here is an urgent plea, ‘do speak softly.’ “aufwacht” = aufwachen, to wake up (separable auf-).

sentences.t: Aber Daumesdick that als hätte er sie nicht verstanden und schrie von neuem „was wollt ihr? wollt ihr alles haben, was hier ist?“
sentences.l: But Daumesdick did as if he had not understood them and shrieked anew, “what do you want? do you want to have everything that is here?”
sentences.i: But Thumbling behaved as if he had not understood them, and shouted afresh, “what do you want? do you want everything that’s here?”
sentences.n: “that als hätte er ...” = tat, als hätte er (thun = 1857 tun): ‘acted as if he had’; the subjunctive hätte marks the unreal comparison. “von neuem” = anew, afresh.

sentences.t: Das hörte die Köchin, die in der Stube daran schlief, richtete sich im Bette auf und horchte.
sentences.l: That the cook heard, who slept in the room next to it, sat herself up in the bed and listened.
sentences.i: The cook, who slept in the room beside it, heard this, sat up in her bed, and listened.
sentences.n: “die Stube daran” = the room next to (the chamber); daran = adjoining it. “richtete sich ... auf” = sich aufrichten, to sit/raise oneself up (separable auf-).

sentences.t: Die Diebe aber waren vor Schrecken ein Stück Wegs zurück gelaufen, endlich faßten sie wieder Muth und dachten „der kleine Kerl will uns necken.“
sentences.l: The thieves however had, for fright, run back a stretch of the way, at last they seized courage again and thought, “the little fellow wants to tease us.”
sentences.i: But the thieves had run back a stretch of the road in their fright; at last they took heart again and thought, “the little fellow is trying to make fools of us.”
sentences.n: “vor Schrecken” = out of / for fright (vor + dative for cause). “Muth fassen” (Muth = 1857 Mut) = to take heart, pluck up courage. “necken” = to tease, make sport of.

sentences.t: Sie kamen zurück und flüsterten ihm zu „nun mach Ernst und reich uns etwas heraus.“
sentences.l: They came back and whispered to him, “now make earnest and hand something out to us.”
sentences.i: They came back and whispered to him, “now be in earnest and hand something out to us.”
sentences.n: “Ernst machen” = to be in earnest, get serious. “flüsterten ... zu” = zuflüstern, to whisper to (separable zu-); likewise “reich ... heraus” = herausreichen.

sentences.t: Da schrie Daumesdick noch einmal so laut er konnte „ich will euch ja alles geben, reicht nur die Hände herein.“
sentences.l: Then Daumesdick shrieked once more as loud as he could, “I will indeed give you everything, just reach the hands in.”
sentences.i: Then Thumbling shouted once more, as loud as he could, “why, I’ll give you everything — only reach your hands in.”
sentences.n: “ich will euch ja alles geben” — the particle ja here = ‘why, of course,’ feigning eager cooperation. “reicht ... herein” = hereinreichen, to reach in (separable herein-).

sentences.t: Das hörte die horchende Magd ganz deutlich, sprang aus dem Bett und stolperte zur Thür herein.
sentences.l: That the listening maid heard quite distinctly, sprang out of the bed and stumbled in at the door.
sentences.i: The listening maid heard this quite distinctly, sprang out of bed, and stumbled in through the door.
sentences.n: “horchende” is the present participle of horchen, ‘listening, harkening.’ “zur Thür herein” (Thür = 1857 Tür) = ‘in through the door.’

sentences.t: Die Diebe liefen fort und rannten als wäre der wilde Jäger hinter ihnen: die Magd aber, als sie nichts bemerken konnte, gieng ein Licht anzuzünden.
sentences.l: The thieves ran off and raced as if the Wild Huntsman were behind them: the maid, however, since she could notice nothing, went to light a candle.
sentences.i: The thieves ran off and fled as if the Wild Huntsman were at their heels; but the maid, since she could make out nothing, went to light a candle.
sentences.n: “der wilde Jäger” is the Wild Huntsman of German folklore, a spectral hunter whose passing was dreaded — proverbial for headlong terror. “als wäre” = ‘as if (he) were,’ subjunctive of unreal comparison. “anzuzünden” = anzünden, to light, kindle (zu-infinitive; separable an-).

sentences.t: Wie sie damit herbei kam, machte sich Daumesdick, ohne daß er gesehen wurde, hinaus in die Scheune: die Magd aber, nachdem sie alle Winkel durchgesucht und nichts gefunden hatte, legte sich endlich wieder zu Bett und glaubte sie hätte mit offenen Augen und Ohren doch nur geträumt.
sentences.l: As she came up with it, Daumesdick made himself, without his being seen, out into the barn: the maid, however, after she had searched through every corner and found nothing, at last laid herself to bed again and believed she had, with open eyes and ears, only dreamed after all.
sentences.i: As she came up with the light, Thumbling slipped off, unseen, out into the barn; and the maid, after searching every corner and finding nothing, at last lay down again and thought she had only been dreaming with her eyes and ears open.
sentences.n: “machte sich ... hinaus” = sich hinausmachen, to make off outside. “ohne daß er gesehen wurde” = ‘without his being seen’ (passive). “glaubte sie hätte ... geträumt” — the reported thought takes subjunctive hätte, as reported speech regularly does in German.

sentences.t: Daumesdick war in den Heuhälmchen herumgeklettert und hatte einen schönen Platz zum Schlafen gefunden: da wollte er sich ausruhen bis es Tag wäre, und dann zu seinen Eltern wieder heim gehen.
sentences.l: Daumesdick had clambered about in the little hay-stalks and had found a fine place for sleeping: there he wanted to rest himself until it were day, and then go home again to his parents.
sentences.i: Thumbling had clambered about among the wisps of hay and found a fine place to sleep: there he meant to rest until day came, and then to go home again to his parents.
sentences.n: “Heuhälmchen” is a double diminutive, ‘little hay-stalks, wisps of hay.’ “herumgeklettert” = herumklettern, to clamber about (separable herum-). “bis es Tag wäre” — the subjunctive wäre marks the still-awaited daybreak.

sentences.t: Aber er mußte andere Dinge erfahren!
sentences.l: But he was to learn other things!
sentences.i: But he was to learn otherwise!
sentences.n: “er mußte ... erfahren” — müssen here carries the sense of what fate had in store: ‘he was to experience.’ “andere Dinge” = other, different things (than a quiet rest).

sentences.t: ja, es gibt viel Trübsal und Noth auf der Welt!
sentences.l: yes, there is much tribulation and want in the world!
sentences.i: yes, there is much sorrow and hardship in the world!
sentences.n: “es gibt” = there is/are. “Trübsal und Noth” (Noth = 1857 Not) is a paired phrase, ‘affliction and distress.’ The narrator’s little exclamation is a moralizing aside.

sentences.t: Die Magd stieg, als der Tag graute, schon aus dem Bett, um das Vieh zu füttern.
sentences.l: The maid rose, when the day dawned, already out of the bed, in order to feed the cattle.
sentences.i: The maid, when day was breaking, was already up out of bed to feed the cattle.
sentences.n: “der Tag graute” — grauen (of daybreak) = to grow grey, dawn. “um ... zu füttern” = ‘in order to feed’ (purpose).

sentences.t: Ihr erster Gang war in die Scheune, wo sie einen Arm voll Heu packte, und gerade dasjenige, worin der arme Daumesdick lag und schlief.
sentences.l: Her first errand was into the barn, where she grabbed an arm-full of hay, and precisely that one, in which poor Daumesdick lay and slept.
sentences.i: Her first errand was to the barn, where she seized an armful of hay — and just that very heap in which poor Thumbling lay asleep.
sentences.n: “Gang” here = an errand, a going-out. “dasjenige” = ‘that one, the very one’ (demonstrative pointing at the armful of hay). “worin” = in which.

sentences.t: Er schlief aber so fest, daß er nichts gewahr ward, und nicht eher aufwachte als bis er in dem Maul der Kuh war, die ihn mit dem Heu aufgerafft hatte.
sentences.l: But he slept so fast that he became aware of nothing, and did not wake sooner than until he was in the mouth of the cow, that had gathered him up with the hay.
sentences.i: But he slept so soundly that he was aware of nothing, and did not wake until he was in the mouth of the cow that had scooped him up along with the hay.
sentences.n: “gewahr werden” = to become aware of, notice (with genitive/object). “nicht eher ... als bis” = ‘not until’ (lit. ‘not sooner than until’). “aufgerafft” = aufraffen, to snatch/gather up (separable auf-).

sentences.t: „Ach Gott,“ rief er, „wie bin ich in die Walkmühle gerathen!“ merkte aber bald wo er war.
sentences.l: “Ah God,” he cried, “how have I got into the fulling-mill!” but soon noticed where he was.
sentences.i: “Oh heavens,” he cried, “how have I fallen into the fulling-mill!” — but he soon saw where he was.
sentences.n: A Walkmühle is a fulling-mill, where cloth was pounded and worked; the churning of the cow’s chewing feels the same. “gerathen” (1857 for geraten) = to get into, land in.

sentences.t: Da hieß es aufpassen, daß er nicht zwischen die Zähne kam und zermalmt ward, und hernach mußte er doch mit in den Magen hinab rutschen.
sentences.l: There it meant to take care, that he did not come between the teeth and be crushed, and afterward he had, all the same, to slide down along into the stomach.
sentences.i: Now it was a matter of taking care not to come between the teeth and be crushed, and afterwards he had to slide down into the stomach along with the rest.
sentences.n: “da hieß es aufpassen” — the impersonal es hieß + infinitive = ‘it was a case of, one had to.’ “zermalmt ward” = was crushed (zermalmen). “mit ... hinab rutschen” — mit = ‘along (with the hay),’ hinab = down.

sentences.t: „In dem Stübchen sind die Fenster vergessen,“ sprach er, „und scheint keine Sonne hinein: ein Licht wird auch nicht gebracht.“
sentences.l: “In this little chamber the windows are forgotten,” he said, “and no sun shines in: a light is not brought either.”
sentences.i: “In this little room they’ve forgotten the windows,” he said, “and no sun shines in — and no candle is brought, either.”
sentences.n: “Stübchen” is the diminutive of Stube, ‘little room,’ his wry name for the cow’s stomach. “die Fenster sind vergessen” = ‘the windows have been forgotten (left out).’

sentences.t: Überhaupt gefiel ihm das Quartier schlecht, und was das schlimmste war, es kam immer mehr neues Heu zur Thüre hinein, und der Platz ward immer enger.
sentences.l: Altogether the quarters pleased him ill, and what was the worst, ever more new hay kept coming in at the door, and the room grew ever narrower.
sentences.i: All in all the lodging pleased him poorly, and, worst of all, more and more fresh hay kept coming in at the door, and the space grew ever tighter.
sentences.n: “gefiel ihm ... schlecht” — gefallen takes the dative (ihm) = ‘it pleased him ill.’ “immer mehr ... immer enger” — immer + comparative = ‘more and more ... tighter and tighter.’

sentences.t: Da rief er endlich in der Angst, so laut er konnte, „bringt mir kein frisch Futter mehr, bringt mir kein frisch Futter mehr.“
sentences.l: Then at last he cried in his anguish, as loud as he could, “bring me no fresh fodder more, bring me no fresh fodder more.”
sentences.i: Then at last, in his terror, he cried as loud as he could, “bring me no more fresh fodder, bring me no more fresh fodder.”
sentences.n: “kein ... mehr” = ‘no more.’ frisch is left uninflected before Futter — a colloquial licence common in Grimm’s spoken lines.

sentences.t: Die Magd melkte gerade die Kuh, und als sie sprechen hörte ohne jemand zu sehen, und es dieselbe Stimme war, die sie auch in der Nacht gehört hatte, erschrack sie so, daß sie von ihrem Stühlchen herabglitschte und die Milch verschüttete.
sentences.l: The maid was just then milking the cow, and when she heard speaking without seeing anyone, and it was the same voice that she had also heard in the night, she took such a fright that she slid down off her little stool and spilled the milk.
sentences.i: The maid was just milking the cow, and when she heard speaking without seeing anyone, and it was the very same voice she had heard in the night, she took such a fright that she slid off her little stool and spilled the milk.
sentences.n: “sprechen hörte ohne ... zu sehen” = ‘heard speaking without seeing.’ “dieselbe Stimme” = the same voice. “herabglitschte” = herabglitschen, to slide down (separable herab-); “verschüttete” = verschütten, to spill.

sentences.t: Sie lief in der größten Hast zu ihrem Herrn, und rief „ach Gott, Herr Pfarrer, die Kuh hat geredet.“
sentences.l: She ran in the greatest haste to her master, and cried, “ah God, Master Parson, the cow has talked.”
sentences.i: She ran in the greatest haste to her master and cried, “oh heavens, master parson, the cow has spoken!”
sentences.n: “in der größten Hast” = in greatest haste. “ihrem Herrn” = her master (dative). “hat geredet” = has talked, spoken (reden).

sentences.t: „Du bist verrückt,“ antwortete der Pfarrer, gieng aber doch selbst in den Stall und wollte nachsehen was es da gäbe.
sentences.l: “You are mad,” answered the parson, but yet went himself into the stall and wanted to look and see what there might be there.
sentences.i: “You are out of your mind,” answered the parson; but he went out to the stall himself all the same, meaning to see what was going on there.
sentences.n: “nachsehen” = to go and look, check (separable nach-). “was es da gäbe” — the subjunctive gäbe (from geben) reports what he wanted to find out: ‘what there might be.’

sentences.t: Kaum aber hatte er den Fuß hineingesetzt, so rief Daumesdick aufs neue „bringt mir kein frisch Futter mehr, bringt mir kein frisch Futter mehr.“
sentences.l: But scarcely had he set foot inside, then Daumesdick cried anew, “bring me no fresh fodder more, bring me no fresh fodder more.”
sentences.i: But scarcely had he set foot inside when Thumbling cried out afresh, “bring me no more fresh fodder, bring me no more fresh fodder.”
sentences.n: “kaum ... so” = ‘scarcely ... when.’ “den Fuß hineingesetzt” = hineinsetzen, to set (a foot) in (separable hinein-). “aufs neue” = afresh, anew.

sentences.t: Da erschrack der Pfarrer selbst, meinte es wäre ein böser Geist in die Kuh gefahren und hieß sie tödten.
sentences.l: Then the parson himself took fright, thought an evil spirit had gone into the cow, and bade them kill her.
sentences.i: Then the parson himself was frightened; he thought an evil spirit had entered the cow, and gave orders for her to be killed.
sentences.n: “es wäre ... gefahren” — reported belief in the subjunctive (wäre gefahren), ‘had gone into.’ “hieß sie tödten” — heißen + infinitive = to bid, order (that she be killed); tödten = 1857 töten.

sentences.t: Sie ward geschlachtet, der Magen aber, worin Daumesdick steckte, auf den Mist geworfen.
sentences.l: She was slaughtered, the stomach however, in which Daumesdick was stuck, thrown onto the dung-heap.
sentences.i: She was slaughtered, but the stomach, in which Thumbling was lodged, was thrown onto the dung-heap.
sentences.n: “ward geschlachtet ... geworfen” — one passive ‘was’ (ward) governs both verbs; the second geworfen (thrown) shares it. “steckte” = was stuck, lodged. “auf den Mist” = onto the dung / muck-heap.

sentences.t: Daumesdick hatte große Mühe sich hindurch zu arbeiten und hatte große Mühe damit, doch brachte ers so weit daß er Platz bekam, aber als er eben sein Haupt herausstrecken wollte, kam ein neues Unglück.
sentences.l: Daumesdick had great trouble to work himself through and had great trouble with it, yet he brought it so far that he got room, but just as he wanted to stick out his head, a new misfortune came.
sentences.i: Thumbling had great trouble working his way through, and great trouble it was; yet he managed so far as to get himself room, but just as he was about to put out his head, a new misfortune befell.
sentences.n: “sich hindurch arbeiten” = to work one’s way through. “brachte ers so weit daß” = brachte er es so weit, ‘brought it so far that.’ “Haupt” is the elevated word for head. “herausstrecken” = to stick/put out (separable heraus-).

sentences.t: Ein hungriger Wolf lief heran und verschlang den ganzen Magen mit einem Schluck.
sentences.l: A hungry wolf ran up and swallowed the whole stomach at one gulp.
sentences.i: A hungry wolf came running up and gulped down the whole stomach at a single swallow.
sentences.n: “lief heran” = heranlaufen, to run up (separable heran-). “mit einem Schluck” = ‘at one gulp / swallow.’

sentences.t: Daumesdick verlor den Muth nicht, „vielleicht,“ dachte er, „läßt der Wolf mit sich reden,“ und rief ihm aus dem Wanste zu „lieber Wolf, ich weiß dir einen herrlichen Fraß.“
sentences.l: Daumesdick did not lose courage, “perhaps,” he thought, “the wolf will let himself be reasoned with,” and called to him out of the belly, “dear wolf, I know for you a splendid feast.”
sentences.i: Thumbling did not lose heart. “Perhaps,” he thought, “the wolf can be talked round,” and he called to him out of the paunch, “dear wolf, I know of a splendid feast for you.”
sentences.n: “mit sich reden lassen” = to be open to persuasion, ‘let oneself be talked to.’ “Wanste” = dative of Wanst, belly, paunch. “Fraß” is the noun for an animal’s food/feed (from fressen) — a fitting, greedy word for the glutton wolf.

sentences.t: „Wo ist der zu holen?“ sprach der Wolf.
sentences.l: “Where is that to be fetched?” said the wolf.
sentences.i: “Where is that to be had?” said the wolf.
sentences.n: “der ... zu holen” — sein + zu + infinitive = ‘is to be fetched, can be got’; der refers back to the Fraß, the feast.

sentences.t: „In dem und dem Haus, da mußt du durch die Gosse hinein kriechen, und wirst Kuchen, Speck und Wurst finden, so viel du essen willst,“ und beschrieb ihm genau seines Vaters Haus.
sentences.l: “In such and such a house, there you must creep in through the gutter, and will find cake, bacon and sausage, as much as you want to eat,” and described to him exactly his father’s house.
sentences.i: “In such and such a house — you must creep in through the drain, and you’ll find cake, bacon and sausage, as much as you care to eat,” and he described his father’s house to him exactly.
sentences.n: “in dem und dem Haus” = ‘in such and such a house’ (a vague placeholder). “Gosse” = gutter, drain. “seines Vaters Haus” = his father’s house (genitive placed before the noun, an elevated order).

sentences.t: Der Wolf ließ sich das nicht zweimal sagen, drängte sich in der Nacht zur Gosse hinein und fraß in der Vorrathskammer nach Herzenslust.
sentences.l: The wolf did not let himself be told that twice, pressed himself in the night in through the gutter and ate in the larder to his heart’s content.
sentences.i: The wolf did not need telling twice; in the night he squeezed in through the drain and gorged in the larder to his heart’s content.
sentences.n: “sich etwas nicht zweimal sagen lassen” = ‘not to need telling twice.’ “drängte sich ... hinein” = sich hineindrängen, to force/squeeze one’s way in. “nach Herzenslust” = to one’s heart’s content. (fraß, from fressen, is the animal’s ‘devouring.’)

sentences.t: Als er sich gesättigt hatte, wollte er wieder fort, aber er war so dick geworden, daß er denselben Weg nicht wieder hinaus konnte.
sentences.l: When he had sated himself, he wanted to be off again, but he had grown so fat that he could not get out again the same way.
sentences.i: When he had gorged his fill, he wanted to be off again, but he had grown so fat that he could not get back out the same way.
sentences.n: “sich sättigen” = to eat one’s fill, sate oneself. “so dick geworden, daß” = ‘grown so fat that.’ “denselben Weg ... hinaus” = out again by the same way.

sentences.t: Darauf hatte Daumesdick gerechnet und fieng nun an in dem Leib des Wolfs einen gewaltigen Lärmen zu machen, tobte und schrie, was er konnte.
sentences.l: On that Daumesdick had reckoned, and now began to make a mighty din in the wolf’s body, raged and shrieked what he could.
sentences.i: This was just what Thumbling had counted on, and now he began to make a mighty din inside the wolf’s body, raging and shouting with all his might.
sentences.n: “darauf rechnen” = to count on it, reckon upon it. “fieng ... an” = fing an (anfangen, to begin; separable an-). “Lärmen” is the old form of Lärm, ‘din, noise.’

sentences.t: „Willst du stille sein,“ sprach der Wolf, „du weckst die Leute auf.“
sentences.l: “Will you be still,” said the wolf, “you are waking the people up.”
sentences.i: “Will you be quiet,” said the wolf, “you’ll wake the people up.”
sentences.n: “willst du stille sein” is an impatient command dressed as a question, ‘will you be quiet!’ (stille = old form of still). “weckst ... auf” = aufwecken, to wake up (separable auf-).

sentences.t: „Ei was,“ antwortete der Kleine, „du hast dich satt gefressen, ich will mich auch lustig machen,“ und fieng von neuem an aus allen Kräften zu schreien.
sentences.l: “Eh what,” answered the little one, “you have eaten yourself full, I will make merry too,” and began anew to shriek with all his might.
sentences.i: “Bah,” answered the little fellow, “you’ve eaten your fill, so I’ll have my fun too,” and he began again to shout with all his strength.
sentences.n: “Ei was” is a dismissive exclamation, ‘bah, nonsense.’ “sich satt fressen” = to eat one’s fill (of an animal). “sich lustig machen” here = to make merry, have one’s fun. “aus allen Kräften” = with all one’s might.

sentences.t: Davon erwachte endlich sein Vater und seine Mutter, liefen an die Kammer und schauten durch die Spalte hinein.
sentences.l: From that his father and his mother at last awoke, ran to the chamber, and looked in through the crack.
sentences.i: At this his father and mother awoke at last, ran to the pantry, and peered in through the crack.
sentences.n: “davon erwachte” = ‘awoke from that (noise).’ “schauten ... hinein” = hineinschauen, to look in (separable hinein-). “Spalte” = crack, chink.

sentences.t: Wie sie sahen daß ein Wolf darin hauste, liefen sie davon, und der Mann holte die Axt, und die Frau die Sense.
sentences.l: When they saw that a wolf was housing therein, they ran off, and the man fetched the axe, and the wife the scythe.
sentences.i: When they saw that a wolf was lodged inside, they ran off, and the man fetched the axe and the wife the scythe.
sentences.n: “hauste” — hausen = to dwell, lodge (often of an unwelcome guest). In the last clause the verb ‘fetched’ (holte) is understood again for the wife: ‘the wife (fetched) the scythe.’

sentences.t: „Bleib dahinten,“ sprach der Mann, als sie in die Kammer traten, „wenn ich ihm einen Schlag gegeben habe, und er davon noch nicht todt ist, so mußt du auf ihn einhauen, und ihm den Leib zerschneiden.“
sentences.l: “Stay back there,” said the man, as they stepped into the chamber, “when I have given him a blow, and he is not yet dead from it, then you must strike in upon him, and cut his body apart.”
sentences.i: “Stay back there,” said the man as they entered the pantry, “when I have dealt him a blow, and he is not yet dead of it, then you must strike at him and cut his body open.”
sentences.n: “dahinten” = back there, behind. “einhauen” = to strike, cut in (separable ein-). “zerschneiden” = to cut apart, cut up (the prefix zer- means ‘to pieces’).

sentences.t: Da hörte Daumesdick die Stimme seines Vaters und rief „lieber Vater, ich bin hier, ich stecke im Leibe des Wolfs.“
sentences.l: Then Daumesdick heard the voice of his father and cried, “dear father, I am here, I am stuck in the wolf’s body.”
sentences.i: Then Thumbling heard his father’s voice and cried, “dear father, I am here, I am inside the wolf’s belly.”
sentences.n: “stecke im Leibe” — stecken = to be stuck/lodged; im Leibe with the old dative -e ending.

sentences.t: Sprach der Vater voll Freuden „gottlob, unser liebes Kind hat sich wieder gefunden,“ und hieß die Frau die Sense wegthun, damit Daumesdick nicht beschädigt würde.
sentences.l: Spoke the father full of joy, “thank God, our dear child has found itself again,” and bade the wife put the scythe away, so that Daumesdick might not be harmed.
sentences.i: The father, full of joy, said, “thank God, our dear child is found again,” and bade his wife put the scythe away, so that Thumbling should not come to harm.
sentences.n: The verb-first “Sprach der Vater” is an old narrative inversion, ‘said the father,’ common in Grimm. “voll Freuden” = full of joy. “wegthun” = wegtun (1857 spelling), to put away (separable weg-). “würde ... beschädigt” — subjunctive of purpose after damit, ‘so that he should not be harmed.’

sentences.t: Danach holte er aus, und schlug dem Wolf einen Schlag auf den Kopf daß er todt niederstürzte, dann suchten sie Messer und Scheere, schnitten ihm den Leib auf und zogen den Kleinen wieder hervor.
sentences.l: After that he drew back his arm, and struck the wolf a blow on the head so that it crashed down dead, then they looked for knife and shears, cut his body open and drew the little one out again.
sentences.i: Then he swung his arm back and dealt the wolf a blow on the head so that it fell down dead; then they fetched knife and shears, cut its body open, and drew the little fellow out again.
sentences.n: “holte ... aus” = ausholen, to draw back the arm for a swing (separable aus-). “schnitten ... auf” = aufschneiden, to cut open. “zogen ... hervor” = hervorziehen, to draw out/forth. (Scheere = 1857 spelling of Schere, shears.)

sentences.t: „Ach,“ sprach der Vater, „was haben wir für Sorge um dich ausgestanden!“
sentences.l: “Oh,” said the father, “what worry we have endured over you!”
sentences.i: “Oh,” said the father, “what anxiety we have suffered for your sake!”
sentences.n: “was für Sorge” = ‘what (a deal of) worry.’ “ausgestanden” = ausstehen, here to endure, go through (Sorge ausstehen; separable aus-).

sentences.t: „Ja, Vater, ich bin viel in der Welt herumgekommen; gottlob, daß ich wieder frische Luft schöpfe!“
sentences.l: “Yes, father, I have got about much in the world; thank God, that I draw fresh air again!”
sentences.i: “Yes, father, I have seen a great deal of the world; thank God I am breathing fresh air again!”
sentences.n: “in der Welt herumkommen” = to get about, see the world. “frische Luft schöpfen” = to draw / breathe fresh air (schöpfen, to draw, scoop).

sentences.t: „Wo bist du denn all gewesen?“
sentences.l: “Where have you then been all this while?”
sentences.i: “Wherever have you been all this while?”
sentences.n: The particle denn again heightens the question, ‘wherever...?’; all here = ‘all about, everywhere.’

sentences.t: „Ach, Vater, ich war in einem Mauseloch, in einer Kuh Bauch und in eines Wolfes Wanst: nun bleib ich bei euch.“
sentences.l: “Oh, father, I was in a mouse-hole, in a cow’s belly and in a wolf’s paunch: now I stay with you.”
sentences.i: “Oh, father, I have been in a mouse-hole, in a cow’s belly, and in a wolf’s paunch — now I shall stay with you.”
sentences.n: Note the two genitives of the possessor: “einer Kuh Bauch” (a cow’s belly) and “eines Wolfes Wanst” (a wolf’s paunch), the noun placed before its head — an old, elevated word order.

sentences.t: „Und wir verkaufen dich um alle Reichthümer der Welt nicht wieder,“ sprachen die Eltern, herzten und küßten ihren lieben Daumesdick.
sentences.l: “And we will not sell you again for all the riches of the world,” said the parents, and hugged and kissed their dear Daumesdick.
sentences.i: “And we shall never sell you again for all the riches in the world,” said the parents, and they hugged and kissed their dear Thumbling.
sentences.n: “um ... nicht wieder verkaufen” = ‘not sell again for (any price).’ Reichthümer = 1857 spelling of Reichtümer, riches. “herzten und küßten” = hugged and kissed.

sentences.t: Sie gaben ihm zu essen und trinken, und ließen ihm neue Kleider machen, denn die seinigen waren ihm auf der Reise verdorben.
sentences.l: They gave him to eat and drink, and had new clothes made for him, for his own had been spoiled for him on the journey.
sentences.i: They gave him food and drink and had new clothes made for him, for his own had been spoiled on the journey.
sentences.n: “ließen ... machen” = to have (something) made (lassen + infinitive). “die seinigen” = ‘his own (clothes)’ (der seinige, the possessive pronoun). “waren ... verdorben” = had been spoiled (verderben).
